titleAr: ما بال عينك ، يا حسان ، لم تنم ،
titleEn: Why Do Your Eyes Not Sleep

verses.sadr: ما بال عينك ، يا حسان ، لم تنم ،
verses.ajuz: ما إن تغمض ، إلا مؤثم القسم

verses.sadr: لم أحسب الشمس تبدو بالعشاء ، فقد
لقد حلفت ، ولم تحلف على كذب ،
verses.ajuz: يابن الفريعة ، ما كلفت من أمم

translationEn: What ails your eyes, O Hassan, that they do not sleep — they cannot close except while swearing a guilty oath? I never thought the sun would appear at evening, yet I have met a sun that illuminates the darkest night. She is the finest of women, and the finest of her people is her father — people of majesty and loyal to their pledges. You have indeed sworn — and it was no false oath — O son of al-Furai'a, what a burden you have taken upon you from the people.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who commits a sin, guilty
the finest, the summit, the branch

context: A short ghazal in which Hassan, addressing himself, marvels at a woman so radiant she is compared to a sun appearing at nightfall. The poem blends self-reproach with admiration for a woman of noble family.

titleAr: ألين ، إذا لان العشير ، فإن تكن
titleEn: I Am Gentle When Companions Are Gentle

verses.sadr: ألين ، إذا لان العشير ، فإن تكن
إذا مات منا سيد ساد مثله ،

translationEn: I am gentle when my companion is gentle, but if he has a madness, my madness came before his. He is close, his evil coming before his good — when they demand the blood-price of me, I pay it. When one of our lords dies, another like him rises to lead — broad-armed in the mastery of leadership, a seasoned chief. He answers the call in great calamities and attends every battle — a trusted man who grows in goodness and is honored.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the battle, the clamor of war

context: A brief fakhr poem extolling the poet's clan — their readiness to pay blood-price, their unbroken chain of leadership, and their valor in battle. Each verse encapsulates a distinct tribal virtue.

titleEn: Chosroes Struck Me with Misery

verses.ajuz: نعم ثم لم تنطق ، ولم تتكلم

translationEn: Chosroes reached me with his wretchedness, though between us lay the plateaus of al-Saman and al-Mutathallam. He bereaved me — may God not grant him success in his affair — of my father, a generous man of little frowning. Let the waters of the two Harithids be effaced — their waters have already been effaced by every great tribe. His watering-place at Hadhar grew desolate after its people departed, though it used to draw water into jars and clay pitchers. I addressed the spring at al-Juba: farewell! It replied yes, then fell silent and did not speak. Dwellings of kings — I saw them in prosperity when the pillar of kingship had not yet crumbled. By my life, a garden between rocky ground and sand-dunes, at a gentle slope where its streams rose above every ravine, — by every lofty edifice and a gathering of revelers with a cup of pure wine not cut short — is dearer to Hassan, if he could reach it, than the dancing-songs of Ghifar and Aslam.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: bereaved me, struck me with grief

context: An elegy lamenting the poet's father, with the desolation of royal seats as a backdrop. Hassan blames the Persian king Chosroes obliquely for the bereavement, then turns to nostalgic images of the Ghassanid royal courts.

titleEn: God Honored Us with His Prophet's Victory
translationEn: God honored us with the victory of His Prophet — through us He established the pillars of Islam. Through us He gave might to His Prophet and His Book, and He gave us might through striking and boldness. In every battlefield our swords fly, splitting skulls from the crests of helmets. Gabriel visits us in our homes bringing the duties of Islam and its rulings. He recites to us the Light therein, firmly ordained — a vow, by your life, unlike any other vow. So we are the first to make lawful what He made lawful, and to forbid for God's sake all He forbade. We are the chosen of all creation, its order and the bridle of every bridle. We wade into the floods of every death and guarantee the chances of the days. We who bind together the strands of affairs by our resolve, and who unravel the twisted ropes of nations. Ask Abu Karib, ask Tuba'a about us, and the people of sacrifice and divining-arrows. Ask those of sound minds about their noblemen on the day of al-'Uhayn, then Hajir, then Rua'm. We protect those we wish to protect and bestow generosity upon those who visit us in need. Our swords turn back the healthy survivors of armies, and we protect the neck of the proudest mighty chieftain. The blows of our swords and our spears have not ceased on every day of struggle and combat — until we left the earth's rough ground strung with our cavalry in ordered ranks. A band fled who had gone too far; had they stood firm, they would not have returned then in safety. If I boast of them through the glory of their ancient deeds — it is the boast of the wise man over the nations.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the mighty chief, the great leader
bands of men, groups without a tribe

context: A major qasida of Islamic fakhr-madih in which Hassan extols the Ansar's role in establishing Islam, receiving Gabriel's revelation, and their unmatched valor in battle. The poem invokes pre-Islamic Yemeni kings (Tuba'a) as witnesses to the tribe's ancient glory.

titleAr: إن ابن جفنة من بقية معشر
titleEn: The Son of Jafna, Remnant of a Noble Kin

verses.sadr: إن ابن جفنة من بقية معشر
لم ينسني بالشام ، إذ هو ربها ،

translationEn: The son of Jafna is of the remnant of a people whose fathers never fed them on blame. He did not forget me in Syria when he was its lord — not at all, nor did he leave me to seek aid from the Romans. He gives abundantly and regards his gift as no more than the paltry gift of one who is criticized. I visited him one day and he drew me close to his seat and gave me the finest drink from the spring.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: remnant, remainder, the best that remains
the pure wine, the finest drink

context: A short panegyric in praise of a Ghassanid king, likely one of the Banu Jafna kings of Syria, whom Hassan visited and received generous hospitality from. The poem praises his generosity and his loyalty to the poet.

titleEn: Whose Is This Faded Dwelling?

verses.sadr: خلاء المبادي ما به غير ركد
وقد كان ذا أهل كبير وغبطة ،
verses.ajuz: إذا الحبل ، حبل الوصل ، لم يتصرم
وإذ مضى من عيشنا لم يصرم

verses.sadr: ضعيف العرى دان من الأرض بركه
فإن تك ليلى قد نأتك ديارها ،
فما حبلها بالرث عندي ، ولا الذي
لعمر أبيك الخير ما ضاع سركم
ولا ضقت ذرعا بالهوى إذ ضمنته ،
ولا كان مما كان مما تقولوا

verses.ajuz: ذوي العلم عنا كي تنبي فتعلمي

verses.sadr: نبيح حمى ذي العز حين نكيده ،
ونحن إذا لم يبرم الناس أمرهم ،
verses.ajuz: نكون على أمر من الحق مبرم

verses.sadr: ونحن إذا ما الحرب حل صرارها ،
ولم يرج إلا كل أروع ماجد ،
فنحن كذاك ، الدهر ، ما هبت الصبا
فلو فهموا ، أو وفقوا رشد أمرهم ،
إنا إذا ما الأفق أمسى كأنما

translationEn: Whose is this faded dwelling whose traces are like the embroidered patterns of fine Sabiri linen? Its campsites are empty, with nothing but three still pools like sitting doves, and a split-trunk tree standing as an ally of decay, and remains like finely-ground dust. Summer winds moisten its withered dry grass upon a standing hulk like a ruined watering-trough. Garments of ruin clothed it after its time, and a dark cloud passed by with torrential crashing rain. Once it held a great household and prosperity when the rope — the rope of union — had not yet been severed. When we were many neighbors there in joy, when a portion of our life had not yet been cut short. Every hastening cloud whose fringes burst open, when the fertilizing winds drive it, pours down. Weak at its fringes, its belly near the earth, gliding like a dark, silent mountain. If Layla's abode has moved far from you and she withheld from the needs of your enamored heart — and she resolved to sever the bond after its union and hearkened to the words of the jealous pretender — my bond with her is not worn away by distance, though she speaks no word. Her love, were she to entrust me with its union — even if friends severed ties — would not be cut off from me. By your father's goodness, your secret has not been wasted in my keeping, that you should repay me with distance and severance. I was not weighed down by love when I harbored it, nor did hidden speech crowd my breast. What they said of me and spread abroad was nothing but unfounded conjecture. If you believe what you hear, then ask the men of knowledge about us so you may be told and learn. Whenever you ask about us you will be told we are noble people of ancient glory. We are the foremost hawks, bold strikers, shouldering a lance whose spine was never marred. By your life, no seeker of generosity comes to our lands for us to turn him away empty and wronged. Nor is the mighty lord, when he plots against us, forbidden to face our spears. Our guest at the time of hospitality is not turned away, nor is our neighbor abandoned in calamities. We open the sanctuary of the mighty man when we plot against him, and we guard our own sanctuaries with straight lances. When people fail to settle their affairs, we are on a matter firmly resolved in truth. Were Mount Radwa weighed against the forbearance of our nobles, our forbearance and Mount Yalamlam would tip the scales. When war unties its rope and bestows upon the milch-camels death and blood — and only every bold, noble man is hoped for, strong of sinew, possessed of glory and dignity — we are the bridle of the two commanders leading to battle when the cowardly trembler does not advance. So we are thus through all time, as long as the east wind blows — we return upon the ignorant with forbearance. If they understood or were guided to the right course of their affairs, we would return to them after hardship with greater bounty. When the horizon at evening seems as if on its two sides the color of dragon's blood reddens — we feed in winter and thrust with spears when war returns like a raging fire. At our tents when we are called upon you find gatherings with every turbaned elder — exalted in the tent's pillar, shielding his honor from blame, of auspicious character, a seasoned man. Generous in all conditions with a wide courtyard — when asked for kindness he does not frown. One who strikes the arrow-butts when it is winter and rushes swiftly to the caller of battle, resolute. Proud-nosed, long-armed, a noble chief, practiced in the clash of armored forearms, bearing battle-scars.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: traces, ruins, remnants of a dwelling
the jealous enemy, one who hides enmity
dragon's blood resin, deep red color

context: One of Hassan's longest qasidas, opening with the traditional atlal (ruined dwelling) motif and ghazal for Layla, then pivoting into a lengthy fakhr extolling the tribe's generosity, forbearance, valor, and nobility. The poem spans 37 verses encompassing classical Arabic poetic themes.

titleAr: أعين ، ألا أبكي سيد الناس ، واسفحي
titleEn: O Eyes, Weep for the Master of Men

verses.sadr: أعين ، ألا أبكي سيد الناس ، واسفحي

verses.ajuz: على الناس ، معروف له ما تكلما

verses.sadr: فلو كان مجد يخلد اليوم واحدا
verses.ajuz: من الناس ، أبقى مجده اليوم مطعما
وأنوم عن جار ، إذا الليل أظلما

translationEn: O eyes, will you not weep for the master of men and pour forth tears? If you exhaust them, then pour blood. Weep for the great one of the two sacred precincts and their lord — known for his generosity among all people whenever he spoke. If glory could immortalize any man today, his glory today would have kept Mut'im alive. He gave sanctuary to the Messenger of God from them, so they became your servants as long as any pilgrim chants the talbiya and enters ihram. Were all of Ma'add asked about him, and Qahtan, or the remaining remnant of Jurhum — they would say: He is the one who fulfilled perfectly the protection of his neighbor and his covenant on the day when covenants were made. The illuminating sun does not rise above them over anyone like him, among them mightier or more noble. Too proud to yield when he refuses, and the noblest of character — and most given to sleep for the sake of a neighbor when night grows dark.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you exhaust it, drain it dry
the two sacred precincts (Mecca and Medina, or Mina and Muzdalifa)
Mut'im ibn Adi, a Quraysh noble
the protection, the pledge of sanctuary

context: An elegy for Mut'im ibn Adi, the Qurayshi nobleman who granted protection to the Prophet Muhammad upon his return from Ta'if. The Prophet himself reportedly said that if Mut'im were alive he would have ransomed all the Badr prisoners for his sake. Hassan's poem acknowledges this noble act of protection.

titleAr: إني ، لعمر أبيك ، شر من أبي ،
titleEn: By Your Father's Life, I Am the Worse

verses.sadr: إني ، لعمر أبيك ، شر من أبي ،

translationEn: By your father's life, I am worse than my father — yet you are better and nobler than your own father. Your sons are all fools, each one afflicted — yet you are worse than your sons and more despicable.
context: A biting two-verse satire (hija) employing a clever rhetorical inversion: the poet admits his own father's weakness only to turn the insult back doubly on the addressee, who is worse than even his foolish sons. A compact example of Hassan's sharp satirical tongue.

titleEn: Inform the Banu Amr of Their Brother

verses.ajuz: شراه امرؤ ، قد كان للشر لازما

translationEn: Inform the Banu Amr that their brother has been bought by a man who was ever committed to evil. He was bought by Zuhayr ibn al-Agharr and Jami' — both of whom had long been accustomed to committing forbidden acts. You gave sanctuary, then when you gave sanctuary you betrayed — and you were at the fringes of al-Raji' devouring. Would that Khubaib had not been betrayed by a pledge of safety, and would that Khubaib had known the character of those people.
context: A poem of denunciation relating to the treacherous killing of Khubaib ibn Adiyy and other companions at al-Raji' (625 CE), when the tribe of Lihyan betrayed the Muslims who had sought safe passage. Hassan castigates the perpetrators by name.

titleEn: Suqa'b, Your Father's Ancestor, a Blacksmith
translationEn: Suqa'b, your father's ancestor, was a blacksmith — base, dwelling in the cleft of the crags. The dark-skinned Hubasha and her brood are counted, asking every man of noble lineage for charity. You call yourselves al-Mughira — that is an injustice — while the old name Daysam is forgotten.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: blacksmith (term of lowly status in early Arabic culture)
the cleft of the rocky crags
old name, here a name of low origin

context: A hija targeting an opponent by attacking the baseness of his lineage — naming a blacksmith ancestor and a dark-skinned mother. The poem accuses them of falsely adopting the noble-sounding name al-Mughira while concealing their base origin.

titleAr: باهى ابن صقعب ، إذ أثرى ، بكلبته ،
titleEn: Ibn Suqa'b Boasted of His Dog in Wealth

verses.sadr: باهى ابن صقعب ، إذ أثرى ، بكلبته ،
verses.ajuz: قل لابن صقعب : أخف الشخص واكتتم

verses.sadr: قل للوليد : متى سميت باسمك ذا ،
verses.ajuz: أم كان ديسم في الأسماء كالحلم

verses.sadr: وإذ حباشة أم لا تسر بها ،
verses.ajuz: لا ناكح في الذرى زوجا ، ولم تئم

verses.sadr: فالحق بقينك ، قين السوء ، إن له
verses.ajuz: كيرا بباب عجوز السوء ، لم يرم

verses.sadr: تلكم مصانعكم في الدهر قد عرفت ،

translationEn: Ibn Suqa'b boasted when he grew rich of his dog — tell Ibn Suqa'b: conceal yourself and stay hidden. Tell al-Walid: when were you given this name of yours, or was the name Daysam once among names like a louse? When Hubasha is a mother who brings no joy — neither married to a noble nor a widow free to choose — then join your blacksmith, the evil blacksmith, for he has a forge at the door of an evil old woman that has not moved. That is your craft throughout time, well known: striking blades and skillfully patching the twisted rope.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the twisted rope, a cheap person

context: A continuation of the hija against the family of Ibn Suqa'b, mocking the boasting of a newly-rich man of low origin whose family trade is blacksmithing. The poem is a biting attack on lineage, occupation, and pretension.

titleEn: Banu al-Najjar Know My Loyalty

verses.ajuz: ولا في عز زهرة إذ تسامي

verses.sadr: ولا في الفرع من ابناء عمرو ،

translationEn: The Banu al-Najjar know well that I defend the clan with the sword. I have left in the arrow of Sahm a festering wound lasting until the day of reckoning and dispute. Do not boast — for old resemblances from the people of Ham have long overpowered you. You are not of the leading lineage of Qusayy, nor in the glory of Zuhra when they competed. Nor in the noble branch of the sons of Amr, nor in the branch of the noble Makhzum. So cease your satire of the Banu Qusayy — for you have already felt the blows of the Banu Haram.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a festering wound, a lasting scar
the day of reckoning, mutual loss
competed in glory, rose in prestige
the descendants of Ham (African origin, used as insult)

context: A poem combining personal fakhr with hija, directed at a rival who claims Qurayshi lineage. Hassan asserts the Banu al-Najjar's fighting reputation and mocks the opponent's lack of authentic noble descent from any of the great Qurayshi houses.

titleEn: Claiming Descent from Qusayy Is Forbidden
translationEn: Know that claiming descent from the Banu Qusayy for one who has no kinship to them is forbidden. You and your claim to the Banu Qusayy are like an unbridled runaway horse. Do not boast — for the Banu Qusayy are the foremost head and the hump of honor. They are the people of fame and noble feats of old, foremost whenever noble lineages are counted. It was they who assigned the stations of Quraysh in Mecca when it had no order. So do not boast of a people you are not of — for your tribe are the mongrel and the base. When the finest of Quraysh are counted, your group slips back into disgrace. Your mother's allotment — if you trace it to a lineage, the noble would disdain it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a runaway horse, out of control
the share, the allotment (often of dubious origin)

context: A hija in the style of nasab (genealogical satire), directed at someone falsely claiming Qurayshi descent. Hassan firmly asserts the true nobility of the Banu Qusayy while exposing the ignoble origins of his opponent's family.

titleEn: I Asked Quraysh and They Knew Well
translationEn: I asked Quraysh — they were well-informed — and all of Quraysh knows you well. Quraysh said, and they did not lie — and the word of Quraysh is binding on you: When stripped to their essence, they are slaves of blacksmiths — your father crouches at his forge. Ask Hisham when you visit him about the rag that is your permanent shame. Did he cook the fat or store it? — your nose is swollen from its smell. A burning coal of disgrace that is fixed upon you — your heart trembles at the thought of it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: they were informed, they had knowledge
a rag, a piece of cloth (insult)

context: A short and biting hija citing Quraysh as collective witnesses to the opponent's base origin as descendants of blacksmiths. Hassan uses the motif of cooking grease and a permanently swollen nose to indicate ignoble work and permanent shame.

titleEn: Quraysh Attained Glory But Banu al-Mughira Fell

verses.sadr: من جوهر من قريش فالتمس بدلا
أو من بني شجع إن كنت ذا نسب

verses.ajuz: عند الثنية من عمرو بن يحموم

translationEn: Quraysh attained the heights of glory, but the Banu al-Mughira slipped away from the glory of the great generous men. They boasted of matters whose rightful owners are people whose lineages from Qusayy are in their throats. The Dar al-Nadwa was inherited from Qusayy, and the standard and the guardianship of the nobles. If you seek a substitute from the essence of Quraysh, look for those who are swift in battle, who advance at the front. Leave the glorious deeds of people that belong in their own homes, and boast of a noble act in the house of Makhzum — or among the Banu Shaja' if you are of genuine free lineage, well-known and attributed. Why did you not protect your mother from disgrace at the mountain pass from Amr ibn Yahmum? The Banu al-Mughira are obscenity in their assembly — they inherited ignorance after disbelief and blame.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: slipped away, became limp, fell short
the great generous ones, noble generous men
the throats (metaphor: it is stuck in their throats)
the assembly house of Quraysh in Mecca
those who rush forward swiftly in battle
those who advance at the front, leaders in battle

context: A hija targeting the Banu al-Mughira, a Makhzumi clan, attacking them for false genealogical boasting. Hassan draws a sharp contrast between Quraysh's true glory (rooted in Qusayy's institutions: Dar al-Nadwa, the standard) and the Banu al-Mughira's baseness.

titleEn: By Abu Sumayya's Life, I Care Not
translationEn: By the life of Abu Sumayya, I care not whether a he-goat bleats or the tribe of Judham speaks. When their goat gives birth they come running: Is it a female kid under your goat, or a boy?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the he-goat (insult comparing men to animals)

context: A brief, scathing two-verse hija equating the speech of the enemy tribe of Judham to the bleating of a he-goat, and mocking their pastoral poverty by depicting them rushing to check whether their goat has given birth.

titleAr: ألم تر أن طلحة من قريش
titleEn: Do You Not See That Talha Is of Quraysh

verses.sadr: ألم تر أن طلحة من قريش
هو الرجل الذي جلب ابن سعد

translationEn: Do you not see that Talha is of Quraysh — reckoned among the great and noble men? His father spent an era in al-Balqa' driving the she-camels in the cover of darkness. He is the man who brought Ibn Sa'd and Uthman from the land of Syria. He is the man spoken of — a stranger between Zamzam and the Station.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the great mighty leaders (plural of qumqum)
she-camels that have passed seven months since calving
the cover of darkness, the side of night
the sacred well at the Grand Mosque in Mecca

context: A brief poem in praise of Talha, a Qurayshi nobleman, commending his lineage and noting deeds associated with his father in Syria. The reference to being 'a stranger between Zamzam and the Station' may indicate Talha's close association with the sacred precincts.

titleEn: When Aquila Is Mentioned with Disgrace

verses.sadr: أبو صيفي الذي قد كان منها ،

translationEn: When Aquila is mentioned in connection with disgrace, the base ones cover their faces from her shames. Abu Sayfi, who was of her, and Makhrama the foundling wanderer. When they are insulted by mention of their mother, they turn away quickly without a word to say.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: covers the face, hides in shame
the foundling, the one of unknown parentage
the wanderer, the one lost in passion or confusion

context: A short hija attacking a woman named Aquila and her offspring, likely directed at known opponents. The poem mocks their inability to defend their mother's honor, depicted as so infamous that even the base are ashamed.

titleEn: O Abu Lahab, Know That Muhammad Shall Prevail

verses.ajuz: سيعلو بما أدى ، وإ كنت راغما

verses.sadr: ولو كنت حرا في أرومة هاشم ،

verses.ajuz: ومأوى الخنا منهم ، فدع عنك هاشما
وغودرت في كأب من اللؤم جاثما

translationEn: O Abu Lahab! Know that Muhammad will rise high by what he has conveyed — even if you are aggrieved. And even if you have denied him and abandoned him alone, following the ignoble fierce one. Had you been truly free in the root of Hashim and in their noble core, you would have prevented the wrongdoings. But your father inherited from among them the quarter of depravity and the refuge of filth — so leave Hashim aside. Hashim rose to noble deeds and glory, but you were left crouching in a pit of baseness.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: abandoned him, failed to support him
the fierce ones, the lions (also used as insult)
root, origin, foundation of a lineage

context: A poem addressed to Abu Lahab (Abd al-Uzza ibn Abd al-Muttalib), the Prophet's uncle who opposed Islam and is cursed by name in the Quran (Surah 111). Hassan accuses him of being a traitor to his own Hashimite lineage and predicts Muhammad's eventual triumph.

titleAr: من سره الموت صرفا لا مزاج له ،
titleEn: Whoever Desires Pure Death Without Mixture

verses.sadr: من سره الموت صرفا لا مزاج له ،
بل ليت شعري ، وليت الطير تخبرني
verses.ajuz: ما كان شأن علي وابن عفانا

verses.sadr: إني لمنهم ، وإن غابوا ، وإن شهدوا ،
ويها فدى لكم أمي وما ولدت ،
verses.ajuz: قد ينفع الصبر في المكروه أحيانا
حتى يحين بها في الموت من حانا

translationEn: Whoever desires pure death without mixture, let him come to the lion's den in the house of Uthman. Those who carry the rings of Yemeni mail-coat, raised above the noses with white coats adorning their bodies. Rather, would that I knew — would that the birds could tell me — what was the affair between Ali and Ibn Affan. They sacrificed a grey-haired man whose brow showed the mark of prostration, who spent the night in glorification and Quran. You will soon hear in their lands: God is Great! O avengers of Uthman! I have approved of the people of Syria as supporters, and of the emir, and of the brothers as brothers. I am of them, whether they are absent or present, until death — and I am called Hassan. O come! My mother and all she has borne are ransom for you — patience in adversity sometimes profits. Tighten your swords with their cords at your belts until the time comes for whoever must die by them. Perhaps you will see one day in envy a caliph of God among you as he once was.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: lion's den, a place of fierce warriors

context: A poem of political madih composed in the context of the First Fitna (civil war), supporting the Umayyad cause of avenging Uthman ibn Affan's assassination (656 CE). Hassan, then elderly, pledges loyalty to the Syrian forces and prays for the restoration of righteous caliphate.

titleEn: O Men, For Tears Stirred by Grief

verses.ajuz: إني عجبت لمن يبكي على الدمن

verses.sadr: يا قاتل الله قوما كان شأنهم
ما قاتلوه على ذنب ألم به ،
verses.ajuz: إلا الذي نطقوا زورا ولم يكن

translationEn: O men! For tears stirred up by grief — I marvel at whoever weeps over ruins. I have seen God's trustee oppressed — Uthman given as a pledge to graves and burial shrouds. May God destroy a people whose purpose was to kill the secure, Muslim, shrewd imam. They did not fight him over any sin he committed — only what they spoke falsely which did not exist. When I remember him, my eyes overflow with four streams of tears running down both cheeks.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: grief, sorrow; also: the way, the path
ruins of a campsite, old dung-heaps
the shrewd, the clever, the discerning

context: An elegy for Uthman ibn Affan, the third Caliph, assassinated in 656 CE. Hassan declares his tears are not for ruins but for the murdered caliph, and asserts that no real crime justified the killing — only invented accusations.

titleEn: The One Who Steals Sips of Wine Submissive
translationEn: And the one who steals sips of wine is submissive to the cup's strike, stealing eloquence from it. I swore to him by it — by the wine Quraysh has made pilgrimage to — and by every clear-mixed wine that has matured: you will keep company with it even if you turn away from it, even if it were I who poured it for him in his beloved cup. It made two rounds, then he said: give me more — and it crept through his neck-veins and fingers. I did not recognize my brother until we had drunk our morning cups three times and the reins slackened. His voice grew soft, his hands relaxed, and he was as if a captive in fetters. He gave away his first garments in exchange for others — without selling, O Umayma, and without humiliation.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who steals secretly, who takes by stealth
we drank the morning cup of wine

context: A wine poem (khamriyya) describing the gradual transformation of a companion under the influence of wine — from stiff and restrained to relaxed and eloquent. Hassan addresses a woman named Umayma at the end, giving the poem a ghazal framing. A vivid depiction of a pre-Islamic/early Islamic drinking scene.

titleEn: The One Holding His Aching Head from Drink

verses.sadr: لما صحا وتراخى العيش قلت له :
فاشرب من الخمر ما آتاك مشربه ،
verses.ajuz: واعلم بأن كل عيش صالح فان

translationEn: And one holding his aching head from intoxication — I called to him while he was overcome, and he made me his ransom. When he sobered up and life eased, I said to him: Life and death are alike. So drink of the wine whatever its drinking brings you, and know that every good life must pass.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: he made me his ransom (expression of affection)
its drinking, the place or time of drinking

context: A short wine-poem (continuation of the previous scene) in which Hassan counsels a hung-over companion with a hedonistic carpe diem philosophy — since life and death are equal, one might as well enjoy the wine while it lasts.

titleEn: If You Ask, We Are a Noble Kin
translationEn: If you ask, know that we are a noble kin — our lineage is the Azd, and our water is Ghassan. High-nosed, with glory and honor — they had pillars like mountains of the great range.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Azd tribe of Yemen, ancestors of the Ansar
Ghassan, the royal Yemeni spring that named the Ghassanid dynasty
high-nosed (mark of Arab nobility and pride)

context: A brief two-verse fakhr affirming the Ghassanid/Azdi origins of Hassan's clan. The Ghassanids traced their lineage to the Azd tribe of Yemen and to the spring of Ghassan. The poem is a compact statement of noble identity.

titleEn: The Bloom of Youth and Black Hair

verses.sadr: ما التصابي على المشيب وقد قل
إن يكن غث من رقاش حديث ،

translationEn: The bloom of youth and black hair — if not enjoyed while possible, it is madness. What is youthful play during grey-haired age when it has diminished? I spent from that both outer and inner aspects. If the talk of Raqash is thin and lean, then what we eat of talk makes it fat. We seized the forelocks of pleasure one day and sent our harvesters to harvest for us. They harvested a delicious, sweet harvest, and satisfied their hunger — yet they do not consume us. And a trustworthy friend I told my soul's secret — he kept it with the faithfulness of a true trustee. He keeps his secret — when we meet, my soul is cooled by the certainty that I would not betray him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the bloom of youth, the prime of youthfulness
the forelocks of pleasure (metaphor for taking control of enjoyment)
one who keeps a secret covered, concealed
his soul was cooled, he was reassured

context: A reflective ghazal on the pleasures of youth and the loyalty of a trusted friend. The poem opens with the carpe diem theme — youth wasted is madness — then weaves in images of feasting, harvesting pleasure, and the comfort of mutual trust between friends.

titleEn: We Used to Say When We Saw Such Beauty
translationEn: We used to say, when we saw one of considerable build and eloquence: You are as if, O one gifted with eloquence and stature, from the Banu Abd al-Madan.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the one gifted, the one endowed with
the Banu Abd al-Madan, a noble Yemeni clan
he is counted, reckoned as considerable

context: A brief madih comparing an eloquent, imposing man to the Banu Abd al-Madan, a noble Yemeni clan known for fine physical bearing. The poem may be a fragment of a longer qasida.

titleEn: Whose Dwelling Grew Desolate at Ma'an

verses.sadr: ذاك مغنى من آل جفنة في الدهر ،

translationEn: Whose dwelling grew desolate at Ma'an, between the upper Yarmouk and al-Khaman? Through al-Qurayat of Balas to Dar Ya — to Faska' — to the nearby palaces. Through the back of Jasim, through the valleys of al-Safr — the abode of tribes and pure-bred camels. That is the dwelling of the mighty one, after its intimates departed and its great-pillared settlements were abandoned. Their mother grieved for them while they grieved for her, on the day they settled by al-Harith of the Golan. Easter has drawn near, and the young maidens swiftly thread coral ornaments. They gather saffron in the openings of their fine linen garments, over which are linen bodices. They need no camel-resin or gum-treatment — they avoid the colocynth of al-Shiryan. That is the abode of the Jafnids throughout time, rightfully as the ages succeed one another. I have seen myself there in firm dignity — my seat and my place beside the crowned king.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: grew desolate, became lonely and empty
bodices, close-fitting garments dyed with saffron
the crowned one, the Ghassanid king

context: A qasida evoking the Ghassanid royal courts of Syria's Golan region, naming specific geographical landmarks around the Yarmouk. The poem's reference to Easter (al-Fash) is significant as it reflects the Christian Ghassanid culture. Hassan recalls his honored place at the court of the crowned Ghassanid king.

titleEn: Yathrib Knows We Are Her Balance
translationEn: Yathrib knows that we in it — when affairs are confused — are its balance. Yathrib knows that we in it — when rain fails — are its rain-stars. Yathrib knows that we in it — when the Aws fear — are their neighbors of protection. Yathrib knows that al-Nabit, in times of commotion, are its humiliated ones. When the Aws see us in our white armor, brandishing our spears, their fires race. They surrender the reins despite themselves, and disobedience descends from their helmets.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Yathrib, the pre-Islamic name of Medina

context: A tribal fakhr poem asserting the supremacy of Hassan's Khazraji clan in Medina over the rival Aws tribe and the Nabit. The poem uses an anaphoric structure (ويثرب تعلم — Yathrib knows) repeated three times for rhetorical force, a common device in Arabic boast poetry.

titleAr: إن سرك الغدر صرفا لا مزاج له ،
titleEn: If You Love Pure Treachery Without Mixture

verses.sadr: إن سرك الغدر صرفا لا مزاج له ،
verses.ajuz: فأت الرجيع ، وسل عن دار لحيان

verses.sadr: لو ينطق التيس ذو الخصيين وسطهم ،
verses.ajuz: لكان ذا شرف فيهم وذا شان

translationEn: If you are pleased by pure treachery without mixture, then come to al-Raji' and ask about the dwellings of Lihyan. A people who all counseled each other to devour the neighbor — so their best man and a he-goat are equals. If the he-goat with his two testicles were to speak among them, he would be a man of honor and standing among them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: al-Raji', site of the famous betrayal of Muslim missionaries
the Lihyan tribe (perpetrators of the al-Raji' massacre)
counseled each other, advised one another
devouring the neighbor (betraying those under protection)

context: A devastating hija of the Lihyan tribe, who betrayed the Muslim missionaries at al-Raji' in 625 CE, selling some to the Quraysh for execution. Hassan equates their best men to he-goats, a severe dehumanizing insult in the Arab tradition of satirical poetry.

titleEn: Convey to Abu Qays a Message

verses.ajuz: ضحى إذ لا تجيب ولا تعين

translationEn: Convey to Abu Qays a message — if he lends it his ear he will understand it clearly. You have forgotten the bridge on the day of Abu Aqil, though you have certain knowledge of our battles. I swear by Hasin — if I do not visit you through the quarters with torches and millstones grinding — the mighty one submits to it when he sees it, and the tenant flees in fear of it. It turns the grey hair of a virgin maiden, and from fear of it the unborn child falls. Watch with your own eyes the sharp swords when the heroes and the crested helmets are raised upon them. They give freely of the heroes' souls with ease, while you are miserly with your own treacherous soul. No weight is in your ear when you are called in the morning while you neither answer nor help. Did we not leave behind us mourning women wailing with ringing cries over your noblemen? You claim to disgrace them without cause — while your own self, if you knew their worth, is the one disgraced. You killed one of ours for a thousand — praise be to God for this manifest victory! That is because a thousand of yours is little against one of ours — yes, and a lie too. May you remain as you were of old, and may we remain as we have always been. Men of al-Najjar surround you like lions of the thicket dwelling in their dens. When we contend with you man for man we are like black bulls clashing. You will not be satisfied by this — know it, O tribes of Aws — as long as a camel moans. I honored you and restrained myself from you, O nobles of the Aws — though restraint is of no use. Out of shame to trade insults with you, and to preserve my honor — for it is a fat nobility. I honored the women and I said: my kin — and now is the time for me to speak or make it clear.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the tenant, the settled resident (of lesser status)
the maiden with firm breasts, a young virgin
the crested helmets; also: stillness, restraint
gatherings of mourning women, funeral assemblies
the moaning, the longing cry (of camels or grief)

context: A long and fierce hija-fakhr qasida directed at the Aws tribe of Medina, addressing Abu Qays ibn al-Aslat, a leading Aws poet and chief. Hassan boasts of the Khazraj's military superiority, recalling past battles, and denounces the Aws's cowardice, treachery, and ignoble lineage while asserting his own tribe's lion-like dominance in Yathrib.
